frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: criterion and sensitivity formalize liberal vs conservative peripheral detection

frameworks.name: subjective inflation
frameworks.one_line: observers overestimate perceptual content in unattended periphery

frameworks.name: higher-order theory
frameworks.one_line: motivates why peripheral percepts may feel richer than they are

core_question: Do observers use liberal detection criteria for peripheral items in a naturalistic driving environment as they do in artificial tasks?
key_assumptions: criterion and sensitivity are separable via SDT
naturalistic driving task preserves peripheral attention demands
false alarms index criterion shifts rather than sensitivity loss
manipulations: fixed vs trial-varying target color for peripheral detection
naturalistic vs artificial stimulus environment (driving game vs lab task)
peripheral location of pedestrian clothing attribute
key_findings: fixed-color peripheral detection yielded liberal criteria and high false alarms, replicating prior lab findings
trial-varying target color shifted observers to conservative criteria with few false alarms but more misses
criterion shifts were quantitatively captured by a detection-theoretic model
interpretations: task demands interact with the criterion observers adopt for peripheral judgments
liberal peripheral detection generalizes from artificial to naturalistic settings only when target identity is predictable
